# Visibility reference fixtures (L4 gate)

These two fixtures are the independent references for the P4 visibility layer
(Sun model, eclipse, twilight darkness, and visible-pass determination). They
are generated by `tool/gen_visibility_fixtures.py` using
**[Skyfield](https://rhodesmill.org/skyfield/) version 1.54** (numpy 2.4.6) and
the **JPL DE421** planetary ephemeris (`de421.bsp`).

> DE421 is used **for the reference only** - Skyfield auto-downloads it to
> generate these fixtures. It is **NOT bundled** in the package: the package's
> Sun model is the analytic Meeus low-precision algorithm and the eclipse test
> is pure geometry, so `satellite_observer` stays ephemeris-free and
> network-free at runtime (ADR-2, NFR-4, NFR-5).

All altitudes are **geometric** (no atmospheric refraction): `altaz()` is called
without temperature/pressure, matching the package, which models none
(ADR-6 / NG5).

## `sun_altitude_ref.json`

For the fixed Warsaw observer (52.2297 N, 21.0122 E, 100 m), the Sun's geometric
topocentric altitude (deg) is recorded every 20 minutes across a full 24 h day
(73 samples), so the set spans full daylight, both twilights, and deep night
(altitude range ~ -22 deg to +53 deg). This gates the analytic Meeus Sun model:
`test/solar/sun_position_test.dart` asserts the Dart Sun altitude matches each
sample to within `0.05 deg` (Meeus low-precision is ~arc-minute, i.e. ~0.01 deg;
`0.05 deg` is a safe honest bound that includes the small frame/aberration
modelling differences).

## `iss_visibility_ref.json`

For a fixed ISS (ZARYA) TLE (NORAD 25544, epoch ~2024 day 122) and the same
Warsaw observer, the generator finds passes (10 deg minimum elevation) across a
5-day window and emits exactly two, each sampled on a 5 s grid across
`[rise, set]`:

* an **evening pass** that is genuinely visible (observer in darkness AND
  satellite sunlit) - here the Sun sits at ~ -15 deg (well below the civil
  -6 deg gate) and the satellite **crosses out of Earth's umbra mid-pass**
  (eclipsed early samples, sunlit later), producing one visible sub-interval; and
* a **daytime pass** that is not visible - the Sun is up (~ +10 deg) and the
  satellite is sunlit throughout, so no sub-interval qualifies.

Each sample carries `is_sunlit` (Skyfield's `satellite.at(t).is_sunlit(eph)`,
which uses DE421), the Sun's topocentric altitude, and the UTC instant. The
fixture also stores the visible sub-interval(s) Skyfield derives (alt < -6 deg
AND sunlit), built from the same sampled booleans the Dart calculator uses.

`twilightDeg` is the civil-twilight gate (-6 deg, ADR-7 default);
`minElevationDeg` is the 10 deg pass gate (ADR-8 default).

### The eclipse-tolerance caveat (why a guard band)

The package's sunlit/eclipsed test uses the **analytic Meeus** Sun direction
(~arc-minute) and a geometric **conical umbra**, while Skyfield's `is_sunlit`
uses the **DE421 ephemeris** Sun. Near the umbra-grazing boundary the
~arc-minute Sun-direction difference shifts the sunlit<->eclipsed *crossing
instant* by seconds to a few tens of seconds. So the tests:

* compare the sunlit boolean per sample and require **exact agreement OUTSIDE a
  +/- 30 s guard band** around any sunlit<->eclipsed transition, and
* compare visible-interval extent within **tens of seconds**, never asserting
  the exact crossing instant.

This is the documented P4 eclipse-tolerance caveat
(`04-implementation-phases.md` section P4). The guard band tolerates only the
crossing-instant shift; everywhere else the booleans must match exactly.

## Why these are fair, independent gates

Skyfield and `satellite_observer` both run SGP4 for the satellite position, so
the propagation is shared. The Sun position and the sunlit test, however, come
from entirely different implementations (DE421 ephemeris vs analytic Meeus +
geometric umbra), so these fixtures genuinely test the new L4 code rather than
re-checking shared math.

## Regenerating

```sh
python3 -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install skyfield
.venv/bin/python tool/gen_visibility_fixtures.py
```
